title: EFT: Motivation Split
description: Three practical clinician-facing slides extracted from the EFT interventions practice deck.

sources.label: Vrana and Greenberg, Overview of Emotion-Focused Therapy

slides.title: Marker: ambivalence with emotional cost
slides.body: Use when one part pushes for change and another protects the status quo.
bullets: Name the two positions as active self-organizations.
Keep both sides emotionally legitimate until their functions are clear.
Look for an integrated next step small enough to enact.

slides.title: Chair work for ambivalence
slides.body: The split softens when each side discovers what the other is protecting.
steps: Give the change side a chair: what do you want and why now?
Give the no-change side a chair: what danger do you prevent?
Deepen each side to primary emotion, need, and feared consequence.
Facilitate direct address until mutual recognition appears.
Ask the whole client for one testable action that respects both sides.

slides.title: Resolution is integration, not compliance
slides.body: A good ending usually has less pressure and more ownership.
items: The protective side is understood before it is asked to move.
The change side speaks from need, not contempt for the stuck side.
The client can name what must be protected during change.
The next step is concrete, relationally supported, and reversible.
